coredata.dc:title: Hydrogen-bond induced enhanced emission ratiometric fluorescent handy needle for visualization assay of amoxicillin by smartphone sensing platform
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lingfei
Yang, Liang
Lin, Dan
Xu, Shihao
Mei, Chunsheng
Yu, Shaoming
Jiang, Changlong
coredata.dc:description: Amoxicillin (AMO) is one of the most commonly used antibiotics, and its abuse in animal husbandry or clinical therapy can pose unpredictable hazards to humans. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a real-time and rapid method to accurately determine AMO content. Here, we designed a fluorescent nanoprobe for qualitative and quantitative AMO determination by using as-synthesized green safe materials of nontoxic red carbon dots (RCDs) and blue carbon dots (BCDs). In the presence of AMO, a reaction promoting hydrogen bonding occurred immediately, resulting in an instant increase in the intensity of the blue fluorescence of BCDs, accompanied by a marked color change from red to blue. For practical application, we designed a nontoxic sensing fluorescent handy needle to directly and quantitatively detect AMO in real samples. This portable and easy-to-use device was demonstrated on a smartphone platform based on 3D printing technology, which offers the advantages of simple production, excellent visualization, fast response, and instant quantitative detection. The device requires an extremely short detection time and has a sensitive detection limit of 2.39 nM. The method presented here enables real-time assessment for food safety, as well as on-site detection under field conditions to track various trace substances for timely health checks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Emission enhancement by induced hydrogen bonds
Fluorescent sensor
Smartphone platform
Visual quantitative sensing
Amoxicillin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of AMO in real samples
The action mechanism of the proposed sensor
Optimization of the sensor and analytical performance for AMO detection
Design and application of a sensing fluorescent handy needle
Construction of a smartphone sensing platform

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM micrograph of BCDs, (B) DLS results for BCDs, (C) TEM micrograph of BCDs+AMO, (D) DLS results for BCDs+AMO. (E) Structural length of amoxicillin. (F) FT-IR spectra of BCDs and BCDs+AMO. (G) UVvis spectra of BCDs and BCDs+AMO.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric probe solution upon exposure to AMO at different concentrations. The AMO concentrations ranged from 0 to 6 μM. (B) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio I448/I625 and AMO concentration. (C) Fluorescence intensity of the probe upon the addition of various interfering substances at 5 μM. The inset shows corresponding fluorescence color photos obtained under 365-nm UV irradiation. (D) A bar graph of the results of the probe selectivity and interference tests.

(A) A structure diagram of the fluorescent sensing handy needle and the definitions of "not exceeding the standard", "critical state" and "exceeding the standard" (B) The color change of the fluorescent probe handy needle when detecting AMO (0–6 μM). (C) The actual test diagram of the fluorescent probe handy needle in the visible light and ultraviolet (UV) regions.

(A) The 3D structure of the accessories of the smartphone sensor platform and a physical image of the smartphone sensor platform; (B) the interface display of the mobile phone sensor platform; (C) the color change of the spinning membrane monitored by the smartphone sensor platform; and (D) the linear relationship between the B/R value and the AMO concentration (0–6 μM).
Schematic of the sensor detection mechanism.
Determination of AMO spiked in real samples.

head.ce:title: Hydrogen-bond induced enhanced emission ratiometric fluorescent handy needle for visualization assay of amoxicillin by smartphone sensing platform

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui 230031, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amoxicillin (AMO) is one of the most commonly used antibiotics, and its abuse in animal husbandry or clinical therapy can pose unpredictable hazards to humans. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a real-time and rapid method to accurately determine AMO content. Here, we designed a fluorescent nanoprobe for qualitative and quantitative AMO determination by using as-synthesized green safe materials of nontoxic red carbon dots (RCDs) and blue carbon dots (BCDs). In the presence of AMO, a reaction promoting hydrogen bonding occurred immediately, resulting in an instant increase in the intensity of the blue fluorescence of BCDs, accompanied by a marked color change from red to blue. For practical application, we designed a nontoxic sensing fluorescent handy needle to directly and quantitatively detect AMO in real samples. This portable and easy-to-use device was demonstrated on a smartphone platform based on 3D printing technology, which offers the advantages of simple production, excellent visualization, fast response, and instant quantitative detection. The device requires an extremely short detection time and has a sensitive detection limit of 2.39 nM. The method presented here enables real-time assessment for food safety, as well as on-site detection under field conditions to track various trace substances for timely health checks.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Emission enhancement by induced hydrogen bonds

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) possess many excellent properties, among which photoluminescence is one important property (Xu et al., 2004). Compared with conventional fluorescent dyes, CDs have excellent fluorescence properties, including photostability and resistance to photobleaching, while also exhibiting water solubility, low/nontoxicity, high stability (Hola et al., 2014; Ruan et al., 2014), prominent biocompatibility and capacitance (Zhang et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2014; Du et al., 2014). Therefore, CDs have considerable application potential to bioimaging (Huang et al., 2014), photocatalysis (Hu et al., 2016), sensing (Hu et al., 2014) and light-emitting devices (Wang et al., 2011). With the widening application of CDs, many difficulties in synthesis have been overcome (Hamaoy et al., 2014). In particular, hydrothermal synthesis using biological precursors offers the advantages of abundance, low cost, nontoxicity and eco-friendliness (Xu et al., 2014; Prasannan and One-pot, 2013; Chaudhary and Bhowmick, 2015; Atchudan et al., 2016). Biological resources are known to be rich in carbohydrates, such as sugars, starches and polysaccharides. Biological compounds contain elements in addition to C, O, and H that facilitate the synthesis of CDs with surface functional groups (such as -OH and -COOH), which can improve water solubility and fluorescence properties. Although the rational use of biological resources to synthesize CDs has been proven to be an effective strategy, the preparation of satisfactory CDs to meet specific requirements remains challenging. Therefore, the search for an economical, simple and environmentally friendly method for preparing carbon dots from renewable biomass precursors has become an active research area.

Amoxicillin is a broad-spectrum antibiotic belonging to the semisynthetic penicillin class that can be used for anti-infective treatment in different systems of humans and animals (De Baere et al., 2002). AMO has adverse effects on the growth of microorganisms. Antibiotics in natural aquatic environments have toxic effects on the organisms therein that disrupt normal growth or reproduction. For example, Andreozzi et al. (2004) studied the toxic effects of trace quantities of amoxicillin on algae in natural water bodies and found strong toxic effects on S. Leopoliensis, a genus of cyanobacteria phylum polycoccus. Note that toxicity or risk assessment of antibiotic pollutants is typically carried out on single antibiotics. However, in actual water environments, antibiotics often occur as a mixture of multiple drugs, which may synergistically produce stronger toxicity than a single antibiotic. The improper and excessive use of AMO in clinical medicine or as a feed additive for animal breeding produces drug residues in various tissues and foodstuffs that pose a grave threat to human health, including allergic reactions and bacterial resistance to antibiotics. Global concerns have been aroused by the increasing presence of AMO residues, the testing of which is currently a mandatory inspection item for animal food in China, the European Union (EU) and North America. Most countries have established maximum residue limits (MRLs) for AMO. The EU sets MRLs for AMO and ampicillin at 50 μg/kg in all animal foods and at 4 μg/kg in milk (Aryee et al., 2022). Thus, AMO detection is necessary to ensure food safety and human health. AMO can be quantitatively determined using traditional detection methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (Hoizey et al., 2002; Gamba and Dusi, 2003), colorimetric techniques (Absalan et al., 2012), kinetic-spectrophotometric data (Garcia-Reiriz et al., 2007) and electrochemical methods (Ensafi et al., 2012; Fouladgar et al., 2011; Biryol et al., 1998; Ojani et al., 2012). However, these methods are generally performed in the laboratory and require expensive instrumentation, toxic solvents, complex sample pretreatment and skilled labor, all of which are costly and time-consuming. Pawar et al. demonstrated for the first time a highly selective and sensitive assay for the detection of amoxicillin in aqueous media based on quantum dot (QD) fluorescence quenching with a detection limit of 5.19 mg/mL, which was slightly high because a ratio fluorescent probe was not used (Pawar et al., 2017). Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a rapid, simple, highly selective, sensitive and visual detection method for AMO ratio fluorescence.

Here, we propose a selective and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on blue-emitting BCDs and red-emitting RCDs for the visual determination of AMO. Initially, the as-prepared sensor displays red fluorescence at 625 nm and weak blue fluorescence at 448 nm under 365-nm excitation. The blue-emissive BCDs serve as recognition signals and turn on in the presence of AMO, whereas the red-emissive RCDs serve as reference signals and are insensitive to AMO. The dramatic color evolution from red to blue enables instant monitoring of AMO by the naked eye under UV light without the use of any complex instrumentation. The detection limit reaches 2.39 nM, which is far below the MRLs specified by the EU. Furthermore, a variety of portable optical devices combined with smartphone integration platforms have been developed considering qualitative and quantitative requirements. The practical application of the designed sensing fluorescent handy needle is demonstrated by monitoring real samples. Overall, the sensing system can realize on-site visual and quantitative AMO detection of real samples, providing a novel concept for the detection of small-molecule pollutants in the fields of food safety surveillance and health monitoring.

Thiram, Dipterex, PbCl2, HgCl2, norfloxacin (NOR), gentamicin (Gen), tetracycline (TC), chloramphenicol (CLP), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), amoxicillin (AMO), phenoxymethylpenicillin (PV), dicloxacillin (DIC), and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Brown granulated sugar and tree leaves were obtained from the local market. Unless otherwise stated, all chemicals were used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) was produced using a Millipore water purification system.

UV−vis absorption was recorded by a Cary UVvis Compact. Fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer. TEM was performed on the samples using a JEM-2100 F transmission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 high-performance electron spectrometer. A Malvern 2000 laser particle size analyzer (DLS) was used in this study. Photos were taken under an AGL-9406 portable UV lamp (365 nm) with a Canon EOS 600D digital camera. A 3D printer (Makerbot Replicator 2X) and a fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (FT200/PicoQuant MicroTime 200), iPhone X (Apple Inc.) were used in this study.

Brown granulated sugar (1.5 g) was mixed with 10 mL of water to produce a transparent solution. The mixture was transferred to a 20-mL stainless-steel autoclave lined with Teflon and heated at 220 °C for 4 h. The reactor was then allowed to cool to room temperature naturally, and the CD solution was dialyzed (cut-off Mn: 500–1000 Da) against deionized water for 24 h to remove small molecules. The solution was stored in a 4 °C refrigerator until use.

The leaves were washed and dried at 60 °C, and 5 g of the leaves were weighed out. At 30 °C, 30.0 mL of an ethanol solution was added to the leaves, and the mixture was stirred for 24 h to extract chlorophyll. The reacted solution mixture was then transferred to a 50-mL stainless steel autoclave lined with Teflon and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. The reactor was then allowed to cool naturally to room temperature, and the CD solution was dialyzed against deionized water (cut-off Mn: 500–1000 Da) for 24 h to remove small molecules. The solution was stored in a 4 °C refrigerator until use.

Fig. S1 shows the novel dual-emission sensing system that was established to detect AMO using biomass-derived carbon quantum dots. To prepare the "nontoxic" sensor, an equal number of RCDs were added to a BCD solution, and the resulting solution was evenly mixed and transferred to a quartz cuvette. AMO was added to the cuvette and incubated for 1 min, producing no change in the fluorescence of the RCDs. However, the fluorescence of the BCDs was clearly turned on. The fluorescence intensity was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence color changes were observed under 365-nm UV light and photographed with a digital camera.

A reaction solution was prepared by adding 20 μL of BCDs, 20 μL of RCDs, 2950 μL of ultrapure water, and an AMO solution (10 μL) at different concentrations in sequence. The solution was reacted for 1 min and transferred to a quartz cuvette. The fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric probe under a single wavelength (365 nm) excitation were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. All sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted in triplicate. Color changes were observed under a 365-nm UV lamp.

To determine the selectivity of the probe, a series of interfering substances (Thiram, Dipterex, Pb2+, Hg2+, NOR, Gen, TC, CLP, CTC, OTC, PV, and DIC), were mixed with the ratio probe instead of AMO. In the actual test, 20 μL of BCDs, 20 μL of RCDs, 2950 μL of ultrapure water, and a 5 μM interfering substance solution (10 μL) were added in sequence. The solution was reacted for 1 min and transferred to a quartz cuvette. The fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric probe under a single wavelength (365 nm) excitation were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. All sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted in triplicate. Color changes were observed under a 365-nm UV lamp. The concentration of the interference ions was 5 μM. For the interference study, 5 μM AMO was added to the probe solution, and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded again.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of AMO in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility and reproducibility of the proposed sensor for practical applications, BCDs were used to detect AMO in samples prepared using standard methods. A lake-water sample was filtered through a 220-nm microfiltration film to remove insoluble impurities and then tested. Eggs were washed before use; the egg whites and yolks were stirred at room temperature for 3 min, filtered through a 220-nm microfiltration membrane to remove insoluble impurities and then used in detection experiments. Fifteen grams of milk were accurately weighed, 35 mL of anhydrous ethanol were added to the milk, and the resulting mixture was mixed well and allowed to stand for 2 min. The supernatant was then filtered through a 220-nm microfiltration membrane to remove insoluble impurities and used in a detection experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The action mechanism of the proposed sensor

ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CDs were structurally characterized by TEM. Fig. 1A shows that the as-obtained BCDs formed a relatively uniform dispersion in water and had an approximate particle size of 2.5 ± 1 nm. The DLS results presented in Fig. 1B show the BCDs ranged from 2 nm to 4 nm in size, which is in accord with the TEM results. AMO addition resulted in the formation of BCDs+AMO, which were also spherical and uniformly dispersed with an average diameter of approximately 3 ± 1 nm. The BCDs+AMO were clearly slightly larger than the bare BCDs, indicating that the AMO molecules successfully attached to the surface of the BCDs. In addition, the DLS results indicate a size range of the BCDs+AMO of 2.5–6 nm (Fig. 1D). We determined the AMO structural length. Fig. 1E shows the lengths of C-C, C-N, and C-O; the AMO length was measured as 1.01 nm, which is consistent with that determined from the TEM and DLS results. The particle size of the RCDs was approximately 2 ± 0.5 nm, which is in good agreement with the DLS measurement (Fig. S2). Fig. 1F shows the FTIR spectra of the BCDs and BCDs+AMO. The vibration of C–OH at approximately 3448 cm−1 in the spectrum of the BCDs+AMO was clearly shifted (Δδ=32 cm−1) from that in the spectrum of the BCDs (3480 cm−1). This shift occurred because the formation of hydrogen bonds can increase the length of chemical bonds such as CO. The stretching vibration of chemical bonds is inversely proportional to the square root of the bond length, and the wavenumber decreases correspondingly (Wang et al., 2021). The FT-IR spectral analysis clearly confirmed the successful formation of BCDs+AMO via hydrogen bonding. To explore the properties of the BCDs before and after AMO addition, UVvis absorption spectra in aqueous solution were recorded and are shown in Fig. 1G. A characteristic absorption peak appears at 268 nm in the BCDs+AMO spectrum, which is significantly shifted (Δδ=13 nm) from that in the spectrum of the BCDs (281 nm). Such a remarkable blue shift of the absorption band is typically assigned to the formation of hydrogen bonds. The formation of inter/intramolecular hydrogen bonds reduces the mechanical parameters of chemical bonds in hydroxyl groups, shifting the absorption frequency to lower wavenumbers (Liu et al., 2004). This result corroborated the formation of hydrogen bonds and the enhanced emission induced by hydrogen bonds as the sensing mechanism. The XPS spectrum provided further evidence of this mechanism based on the elemental contents and surface groups of the BCDs. Fig. S3 shows no discernible change between the spectra of the BCDs before and after the reaction due to the small photoionization interface of H in the hydrogen bond, the weak signal, the easy transfer of 1 s electrons, and the absence of inner electrons (Figure. S3). The changes in the zeta potential and fluorescence lifetime resulting from the addition of AMO to the BCDs can be seen in Figs. S4 and S5. Fig. S4 shows that the BCDs were initially positively charged and clearly became negatively charged after AMO addition, which proves that the electron donating group of the BCDs reacted with the functional group of AMO to change the BCD charge. Figure. S5 shows that the fluorescence lifetime of the BCDs also changed significantly after the addition of AMO (from 2.43 ns to 2.60 ns). The addition of AMO increased the BCD fluorescence lifetime, and the enhanced fluorescence intensity also facilitated the sensing mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the sensor and analytical performance for AMO detection
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to applying the proposed method to detect AMO, the excitation wavelength of the probes, pH, incubation time, temperature and concentration ratio (RCDs/BCDs) were optimized to achieve the best sensing performance. Fig. S6 shows that 365 nm is the optimal excitation wavelength. Next, we studied the individual probes. Fig. S7 shows that the fluorescence of the BCDs is weak in the absence of AMO and enhanced by AMO addition. Fig. S8 clearly shows that gradually increasing the AMO concentration produces no discernible change in the fluorescence of the red RCDs but significantly enhances the fluorescence of the blue BCDs. Fig. S9 shows the effect of the pH on the detection performance of the nanoprobe after AMO addition. To the best of our knowledge, reactions to promote hydrogen bonding are generally carried out under acidic conditions (Zhai et al., 2021). At pH below 6, the BCDs+AMO is almost stable. Alkaline conditions (pH >6) result in a decline in the fluorescence intensity of the BCDs. This result is obtained because alkaline conditions do not facilitate the formation of hydrogen bonds and reduce the reaction efficiency, resulting in slow recovery of blue fluorescence after NO2 - addition (Ren et al., 2018). Here, to realize accurate detection, a subsequent test was carried out at pH= 4. Fig. S10 shows that the maximum fluorescence intensity ratio of I448/I625 was obtained within 1 min of AMO addition (Fig. S11) and remained stable during the following 360 min. There was no significant change in the fluorescence intensity ratio over the temperature range of 4–50 °C (Fig. S12), indicating high sensor stability. Considering practical applications, a room temperature of 25 °C was selected as the optimal temperature for detection. To enable visual AMO detection, the response of the sensing system with different fluorescence intensity ratios I448/I625 to AMO was carefully studied. Added AMO did not change the fluorescence intensity of RCDs but caused the fluorescence intensity of BCD to decrease gradually. The results of experiments on the sensor with different probe fluorescence intensity ratios I448/I625 showed that a ratio of 1/8 produced the best wide-chroma color evolution effect, as observed by the naked eye (Fig. S13).

Quantitative detection of AMO based on a ratiometric strategy was performed under the optimum conditions. Fig. 2A shows that with increasing AMO concentration, the blue emission at 448 nm gradually increases to a bright blue-emissive fluorescence, while the red emission at 625 nm remains unchanged, resulting in a continuous change in color from red to violet to blue. The fluorescence intensity ratio I448/I625 of the BCDs-RCDs sensor was proportional to the AMO concentration over the range of 0–6 μM (y = 1.256x-0.6494, R2 =0.9816, Fig. 2B). The LOD for AMO was calculated as 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation and k is the slope of the regression equation (Wang., n a), to be as low as 2.39 nM. The as-proposed sensor also has an excellent detection limit and response time compared to those reported in previous studies (Table S1).

The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the assay were evaluated using other analytes. Experiments were performed to assess the influence of potential foreign substances, including common heavy metal ions (Pb2+ and Hg2+), representative pesticides (Thiram, Dipterex), and seven antibiotics (NOR, GEN, TC, CLP, CTC, OTC, PV, DIC, and AMO), that can affect the fluorescence intensity. Fig. 2C shows no significant signal changes were observed after the addition of these interfering substances. Moreover, the coexistence of these interfering species resulted in negligible interference to AMO sensing. The corresponding photos show that AMO induced a significant fluorescence color change under UV light, unlike the interfering species. Fig. 2D shows the anti-interference performance of the sensing system. The effect of adding interfering substances to the system containing the probe+AMO was observed. The addition of interfering substances has no discernible influence on the entire reaction system and does not affect the probe-AMO system, which confirms the accuracy and anti-interference ability of the sensor for AMO detection. Therefore, fluorescent sensors provide a promising method for the highly selective and qualitative identification of AMO with potential applications to various fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and application of a sensing fluorescent handy needle
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of using the highly stable well-designed sensing fluorescent device in a visual practical visual assay device for AMO detection was also investigated. Fig. 3A shows the basic structure of the fluorescence sensing handy needle, including a "needle" in the front, a "syringe" in the middle, an "inner helix", a "nut" in the tail and a "needle tail pusher". Our designed hand-held needle directly sucks liquid samples for detection (Fig. 3B). After the needle sucked in the test liquid, we spun down the probe solution from the helical column inside the needle tube, and the response was initiated by fully mixing the probe solution into the liquid samples. Variations in the probe color (from red to purple to blue) with increasing AMO concentration (from 0 to 6 μM) were clearly observed under a 365-nm UV lamp. Thus, the solution concentration was distinguished by color and divided into "not exceeding the standard", "critical state" and "exceeding the standard". Fig. 3C shows how the handy needle operates in practice and the real detection performance. During the test, after the "needle port" contacted the liquid samples, we pressed the "needle tail pusher" to absorb the solution. Then, the probe solution was injected to enable complete reaction, and the visual detection result was reflected in a timely manner in the transparent window of the needle tube. Therefore, the nanoprobe-based sensing fluorescent handy needle maintains efficient detection performance without complex operation procedures and offers the advantages of portable, real-time, and nontoxic rapid detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a smartphone sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The use of most analytical methods based on test strips in previous studies have been limited to semiquantitative monitoring based on the ability of the naked eye to distinguish slight color changes. It is difficult for modern rapid detection technologies to meet the requirements for accurate and quantitative monitoring. In most cases, the fluorescence sensing process involves the use of a hand-held UV lamp and dark environmental conditions, which makes it difficult to achieve portability and on-site real-time detection. To solve these problems, a portable optical-sensing platform consisting of a removable smartphone-linked device was built in this study using 3D printing technology and an app program (RGB Test, Zbigniew Osada). A 3D model of the accessories was drawn using relative software, and the specifications were imported into a 3D printer. Fig. 4A shows the main parts of the accessory device, the mobile phone holder, the socket for fixing the small UV lamp, and the sample slot. The device can be combined with a smartphone to ensure a stable and reliable operating environment. The smartphone-based fluorescence sensing platform for AMO quantitative detection is implemented through three steps: image acquisition, signal detection, and quantification analysis. Exposing the sample slot to AMO at different concentrations resulted in a rapid change in the fluorescence color (Fig. 4C). Color photos of the fluorescence were quickly captured and accurately converted to corresponding RGB values by the app, as shown in Fig. 4B. The concentration was calculated and is presented clearly in Fig. 4D. Here, the blue-channel/red-channel (B/R) value exhibits an excellent linear relationship with the AMO concentration in the 0–6 μM range, with a linear coefficient R2 = 0.978. Therefore, the B/R value can be used for quantitative detection of AMO with a detection limit of 7.81 nM. These results show that the simple, portable and real-time detection platform method realizes ultrasensitive detection for AMO and provides technical support for public health and food safety protection.

The practicability and reproducibility of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was evaluated using real samples. The evaluation was carried out using standard addition methods and by performing recovery experiments. AMO was added in a series of concentrations (1.0, 3.0, and 5.0 μM) to real samples of lake water, eggs, and milk, and the results are shown in Table 1. The calculated recovery values based on the detection results ranged from 94.0 % to 107.0 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) ranged from 0.660 % to 2.83 %, indicating good accuracy and reliability. Table S2 shows the results of using the sensing fluorescent handy needle and smartphone sensing platform to test the real samples. The calculated recovery values based on the detection results ranged from 94.0 % to 104 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) ranged from 0.650 % to 4.31 %, indicating good accuracy and reliability. There are no significant differences between the results shown in Table S2 and Table 1. The results show that the developed sensing system is suitable for practical application to real samples.

In conclusion, a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe was constructed using biomass-derived blue- and red-emitting CDs for the detection of AMO. Upon AMO addition, the red fluorescence of RCDs remained unchanged, while the BCDs exhibited gradient fluorescence enhancement due to enhanced emission induced by the formation of hydrogen bonds, resulting in a broad change in the fluorescence color from red to blue, indicating a high sensitivity with a detection limit of 2.39 nM. These results were used to design a nontoxic ratiometric fluorescent sensing needle that utilizes fluorescence color discrimination for sensitive, rapid, in situ and visual monitoring of AMO. In addition, a smartphone sensing platform based on nontoxic dual-emission probes was successfully realized, validating the application potential for quantitative AMO identification. The designed device is cost-effective, portable, and reliable for on-site analysis of AMO in food and the environment. Strategies for portable and efficient devices are optimized in this study to develop an eco-friendly sensing platform for regulation of environmental, food, and public safety.

Lingfei Li: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Liang Yang: Investigation, Project administration, Validation, Writing – original draft. Dan Lin: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shihao Xu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Chunsheng Mei: Investigation, Methodology. Shaoming Yu: Investigation, Project administration, Validation. Changlong Jiang: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Validation.

Amoxicillin (AMO) is a broad-spectrum antibiotic belonging to the semi-synthetic penicillin class, which can be used for anti-infective treatment in different systems of humans and animals. However, due to the abuse and improper use of AMO, drug residues will be discharged into the soil and water environment, causing serious pollution to the environment, further aggravating bacterial resistance, and posing a serious threat to human health. The increase in AMO residues has attracted global attention, and its residue detection has now become one of the mandatory inspection items for animal food in China, the European Union and North America.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos, 22274158, 21876175), National Key Research and Development Program (2021YFD2000200), Key Re-search and Development Program of Anhui Province (202004d07020013), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Provincial (2208085MB30).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2022.130403.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
